r
a sliding modes [6], also the time derivative of  the sliding
variable is forced to zero,  i.e. σ = σ˙ = 0,  thus enabling
for robust regulation of the systems also with relative degree
two between the sliding variable and control value. Here
it  is worth recalling that  the second-order SM controllers
are seen to belong to a more general class of higher-order
sliding mode control, actively developed over the last two and
a half decades for  the uncertain systems with the relative
degree  two and higher,  see e.g.  [4],  [5]  and references
therein. Applications and experimental case studies with the
use of the SM control (and also estimation) techniques are

enchmarking of
al Sliding Mode Control
uderman
numerous. Here to mention just a few of the recent, the SM
applications can be found in e.g. the temperature control of
chemical fluids for silicon wafer production [7], in the states
estimation for frequency regulation and economic dispatch
in the power grids [8],  in the control of hydro-mechanical
drives [9], and many others.
The so called sub-optimal SM control was proposed in
[10] as a second-order SM technique which does not require
σ˙  for  a discontinuous  control  commutation. Recall  that
a single commutation at  the well-defined time instant  in
an unperturbed system results  in the time optimal bang-
bang control, cf. [11]. In turn, the sub-optimal SM control
[10]  converges  robustly  to (σ, σ˙ ) origin  in presence of
the bounded perturbations, while executing a commutating
control  sequence u(t) of an increasing frequency. For a
detailed survey of the properties, generalizing extensions, and
applications of the sub-optimal SM control we refer to [12].
Since the sub-optimal SM control u(t)  is continuously
commutating between two sign-alternating discrete values
∀ t > 0, actually like any discontinuous SM control, the cor-
responding cumulative energy consumption is proportional
to the execution time, or at least to the convergence time if
the control can be switched off after reaching the origin.
Recently,  an energy saving extension of  the sub-optimal
SM control was introduced in [13], which allows for  the
well defined u = 0 phases during the convergence. The
original sub-optimal SM control was modified in [13] by
introducing an additional switching threshold which enables
for a mandatory u = 0 phase between two consecutive
extrema of the sliding variable, cf. with [12]. This implied the
control value to be in the finite set u ∈ {−U, 0, U}, while
the class of perturbed systems with relative degree two and
discrete bounded control, i.e. max |u| = U, were targeted.
In this paper,  the energy-saving sub-optimal SM control
[13]  is experimentally benchmarked in comparison with a
classical  terminal second-order SM control. The latter as-
sumes u ∈ {−U, U} and provides a finite-time convergence
while compensating robustly for the bounded perturbations
|d(t)| < U,  see e.g.  [14]. Worth noting is also that  the
energy-saving sub-optimal  SM control  [13]  is  evaluated
experimentally for the first time, while the targeted electro-
magneto-mechanical  system [15]  represents a sufficiently
challenging application case with regard to the uncertainties,
disturbances, and sensor noise.
The rest of  the paper  is as follows.  In Section II, we
provide the necessary preliminaries of the energy-saving sub-
optimal SM control [13] and the benchmarking terminal SM
control with the corresponding second-order sliding surface.

In this section, after specifying the class of the systems
under consideration, the energy-saving sub-optimal SM con-
trol [13], which constitutes an extension of the original sub-
optimal SM control [10], is summarized for convenience of
the reader. Then,  the so called terminal SM control  [17]
is recalled, while the emphasis is on design of an optimal
second-order sliding surface discussed in [14]. By the end,
the appearance and parameters of chattering, due to addi-
tional (parasitic) actuator dynamics, are briefly addressed.
The class of uncertain dynamic systems under considera-
tion is the one which can be assigned with the well-defined
sliding variable

The  imposed β1, β2  constraints  (14)–(16)  have a well-
interpretable graphical visualization as shown in Fig. 1. The
set of admissible β1, β2 values is inside of a color-shadowed
triangle. The β1 = β2 edge recovers the original sub-optimal
SM control. Obviously,  the D/U ratio determines the size
of the admissible {β1, β2} set.  It also determines whether
the negative β2-values are allowed, while this can lead to a
twisting mode during the convergence, see [13]. Recall that
it is on the interval (β2σM, β1σM) where the control value
is switched off,  i.e. u = 0, and thus operates as energy-
saving. It appears at each reaching cycle,  i.e. between two
consecutive extremal values,  i.e. σMi  and σMi+1 , and that
throughout the entire convergence period.

For a detailed analysis of the convergence and efficiency
of the energy-saving sub-optimal SM control, an interested
reader is referred to the full paper [13].
B. Second-order terminal SM control
For second-order systems,  i.e. with the dynamic states
x1(t) and x2(t) = x˙ 1(t), the so called terminal SM control
(cid:0)  (cid:1)
u(x1, x2)  = −Kt sign σ(·) ,  (20)
2
σ(x1, x2)  = x1 + δ x2 sign(x2),  (21)
was proposed  in [17], while a quadratic sliding surface
σ(·) similar  to (21) was exemplified already before,  see
[6, Sec. 4]. Remarkable in (21) is that for an appropriately
selected δ,  the parabolic sliding surface coincides with the
time-optimal state trajectory of the well-known Fuller’s prob-
lem [11]. This has been further analyzed for the second-order
motion systems in [14] so that for
δ = α/U,  (22)
where U ≡ max |u| is the maximal control amplitude,  the
α = 0.5 value represents the boundary layer of the terminal
mode, cf.  [14, Fig. 2]. That means for 0 < α < 0.5,  the
control  (20),  (21) changes to the so-called twisting mode,
see e.g.  [4],  [5] for details. Both modes,  the terminal and
twisting, provide a global finite-time convergence of  the
(x1, x2) trajectories to the origin. Also recall  that  for  the
control (20), (21) remains globally finite-time convergent in
case of the bounded perturbations |d(t)| < D, the following
control-gain condition Kt > D must be fulfilled.
With regard to the above basics,  the used second-order
terminal SM control is given by
u¯  = −U sign (σ),  (23)

avoidance of the twisting mode must be guaranteed. Also

A. Voice-coil based linear actuator
The benchmarking experimental system in use is a voice-
coil based actuator, see e.g. [20] for basics, with one linear
degree of freedom (DOF). The laboratory view of the actua-
tor is shown in Fig. 4. More technical details, including the
system parameters, can be found in [15]. Most relevant  to

be noted is that the sampling rate fs of the real-time control
is relatively high, set to 10 kHz. And the time derivative of
the measured output displacement x(t) is obtained by the
discrete time differentiation combined with a low-pass filter
(LPF). The cutoff frequency is 1 kHz. Recall  that x˙ (t) is
required for constructing the sliding surface σ, cf. (24).
Fig. 4.  Voice-coil based linear actuator with one DOF in x coordinates.
The modeled system dynamics is given by
mx¨(t)  =  v(t) +G+ d(t),  (28)
µv˙(t)  = Ku(t) − v(t).  (29)
The overall mass of the actuator mover is m = 0.538 kg.
The  input gaining factor, which takes  into account both
the electro-magneto-mechanical coupling constant and the
resistance of the anchor coils, is K = 3.28 N/V. The constant
gravity term is G = 5.27 N, while the cumulative unknown
(cid:12)  (cid:12)
bounded disturbance is 0 < (cid:12)d(t)(cid:12) < D. Estimated from
various open- and closed-loop experiments, the upper bound
of disturbances is set  to D = 1 N. The time constant of
the electro-magnetic circuit of the actuator µ = 0.0012 sec
is taken from the manufacturer’s technical data sheet. The
available signals are the input voltage u(t) (given in V) and
the output relative displacement x(t) ≡ x1(t) (given in m).
B. Noisy system states
The measured output signal is subject to the noise. This is
due to a contactless sensing of the relative displacement by
means of the inductive sensor, while its nominal repeatability
is ±12 micrometer. When the control signal is off, i.e. u(t) =
0,  the background noise of the output  is still visible. The
distribution of an exemplary measured output x(t) at zero
input is shown in Fig. 5 (a). While zero mean-value can be
assumed, a nearly normal distribution can be recognized, and
−5
the evaluated standard deviation is 4.58×10  m. To get an
idea about additional process noise, i.e. disturbances, one can
inspect a fragment of the measured output x(t) at the applied
constant input u(t) = const, see Fig. 5 (b). One can recog-
nize a harmonic pattern in the output relative displacement,
caused by a electro-magnetic cogging of the stator-armature
structure. Worth noting is that the disturbance d(t) includes

The experimental results demonstrated below are obtained
by using the control value
(cid:0)  (cid:1)
u(t) = usm x, x˙  + ugr,  (31)
where ugr = 1.61 V is compensating for the constant gravity
term, cf. (28), and the sliding-mode control usm = {u¯, u˜}
is either the terminal (23) or the energy-saving sub-optimal
one (12), both using the same second-order sliding surface
(24). The magnitude of  the switching control  is assigned
to U = 0.8 V,  that  is about 2.5 times higher  than the
corresponding upper bound of disturbances, cf. with D in
section III-A and [13]. The design parameter of the terminal
sliding surface is assigned as α = 1.2,  thus preserving the
terminal algorithm from the twisting mode with respect  to
the perturbations upper bound D. The threshold parameters
of the energy-saving sub-optimal control are assigned to be
β1 = 0.85 and β2 = 0.1, respectively, cf. [13].
The control experiments were conducted for the reference
step xr = 0.015 m,  starting from the same zero relative
position. Note that through a transformation of coordinates

Below, we will however continue to use x instead of x ,

For evaluation of  the control  energy consumption,  the
following integral metric is used
(cid:90)
E =  |u(t)|dt.  (32)
Recall that for a continuously switching SM control |u| =
U, while  for  the energy-saving sub-optimal SM control
|u| = {U, 0}. Therefore, the metric (32) reflects the overall
time when the control signal  is on and, hence,  the energy
consumption during the controlled operation. The measured
output response of both SM controllers under benchmarking
is  shown Fig.  6 (a). Both controls  reach  the  reference
equilibrium in finite time and show the same steady-state
accuracy, while the reaching time of the terminal SM control
is slightly superior comparing to the energy-saving sub-
optimal SM control, cf.  [13]. This is not surprising since
both controllers maintain the system on the same sliding
manifold (24), while the terminal SM controller is always
on. The benefit of the energy-saving sub-optimal SM control
becomes visible when inspecting the evaluated metric (32)
depicted in Fig. 6 (b). Not only during the reaching phase, i.e.
on the time interval bounded by approximately t < 0.4 sec,

the second-order terminal SM control in regard to the used
actuator with additional (parasitic) dynamics.
V. CONCLUSIONS
The energy-saving sub-optimal SM control, recently pro-
posed in [13], was for the first time evaluated experimentally
and benchmarked with a conventional discontinuous SM
control, both operating on the same terminal sliding surface.
The surface ensures both SM controllers to operate in the
second-order sliding mode, while the latter appears in a finite
time. The real experimental system with the measurement
and process noise, and without sensing of the time derivative
of  the output  and of  the sliding variable,  is considered.
The system is modeled in a simplified way by a perturbed
double integrator. The latter is with the known upper bound
of  the matched perturbations and an additional  (parasitic)
first-order actuator dynamics. The same switching control

magnitude and the designed terminal sliding surface allow
for a fair comparison of both, the conventional discontinuous
(terminal) and energy-saving sub-optimal SM controllers.
The integral of the absolute control value is evaluated as an
adequate energy consumption metric which is proportional to
the overall time when the control with a constant magnitude
was on. The provided experimental case study confirms the
applicability and efficiency of the energy-saving sub-optimal
SM control [13].
